title: Choosing a pig breed for the smallholding
subtitle: Traditional breeds vs commercial crosses for the outdoor, small-scale keeper.
excerpt: The pig breed you choose sets the pace of everything else: how fast the pigs grow, how much fat they carry, how easy they are to handle, and what the finished pork tastes like. Traditional UK breeds grow slowly and marble well. Commercial crosses grow fast and lean. Here's how to match the breed to your land, your market, and your timescale.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. Rare Breeds Survival Trust breed profiles. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig chapter. AHDB Pork, breed characteristics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young pig that has been weaned from its mother, usually at around eight weeks, and is ready to move to a growing pen or a new holding.
A pig kept specifically to produce bacon and larger cuts; typically slaughtered at a heavier weight than a pork pig.

content.text: There are two broad choices for a smallholder: a traditional British rare breed, or a commercial cross. Both produce excellent pork; they differ in growth rate, fat cover, hardiness, and finished price. The choice depends on whether you want the fastest route to the freezer or the best fat-to-lean ratio for cured products and slow cooking.

The Tamworth, Berkshire, Large Black, Oxford Sandy and Black, and Gloucester Old Spot are all suited to outdoor keeping on pasture. They're hardy enough to winter outside with adequate shelter, have good temperaments, and root enthusiastically, which makes them excellent land clearers. They grow slowly compared to commercial crosses, a Tamworth

typically takes 26,30 weeks to reach slaughter weight, compared to 22,24 weeks for a commercial cross. The carcass carries more fat, which means better marbling for roasting pork, better crackling, and excellent lard.

Landrace-Large White crosses and their derivatives are the commercial standard. They grow faster, produce a leaner carcass, and convert feed to meat more efficiently than traditional breeds. They're perfectly adequate for smallholder use but are not as robust in adverse weather conditions and don't have the same outdoor foraging drive. If your primary aim is pork for the freezer as quickly as possible, a commercial cross
from a reliable local source is a practical choice.
Tamworth, lean, long-bodied, excellent outdoor pig; ginger colouring suits woodland and pasture; good for bacon
Berkshire, smaller-framed, black with white points; docile, matures quickly for a rare breed; excellent pork flavour
Oxford Sandy and Black, calm and good-natured, easy to handle, good for beginners; finishes more slowly than a commercial cross
Gloucester Old Spot, traditional apple-orchard pig; big-framed, heavy fat cover, good for lard production
Large Black, hardiest outdoor pig; docile, long floppy ears (limited forward vision makes them easy to lead)

Buy weaners from a breeder who keeps their pigs outdoors, on a worming programme, and who will let you visit before buying. A weaner that has been kept indoors in a large barn and is suddenly moved outdoors takes longer to settle. Check the holding has a current CPH number and that the vendor completes the movement documentation correctly.
